comparisons.reasoning: Implementations A and B are structurally identical in how they organize the simulation core (`runMonteCarlo`), NPV calculation (`npv`), and randomness utilities, so neither offers a clearer extension point than the other for introducing a different model. In both cases, adding a new model would require the same kind of refactor: separating the generic Monte Carlo engine (generating cash-flow paths and statistics) from the specific NPV-based payoff calculation, then plugging in alternative model functions. Because the code boundaries, responsibilities, and coupling are the same, the amount of existing code to modify versus new code to add, as well as the resilience to new requirements, would be effectively identical for A and B.

Implementations A and B are identical across all provided files (`main.js`, `npv.js`, `random.js`, `simulation.js`), so they satisfy the task description to the same extent. Both provide a Monte Carlo NPV simulation using random cash flows, compute distribution statistics (mean, std dev, quantiles) and an explicit probability of positive NPV, aligning with the intent to estimate probability of project success. They also respect the stack constraint (plain DOM usage in `main.js`, logic in separate modules) and avoid documentation/comments as requested. Neither snippet shows tests or HTML, so they are equally incomplete with respect to those explicit requirements, making the overall faithfulness a tie.

Implementations A and B are textually identical across all files (same functions, structure, and validations), so they introduce the same number of lines, the same abstractions (`npv`, `presentValue`, `randomNormal`, `randomNormalWith`, `runMonteCarlo`, `sortNumbers`, `quantile`, formatting and parsing helpers in `main.js`), and the same constructs that exist only to support others (e.g., `sortNumbers` solely to support `quantile`, and the Box–Muller cached state to support `randomNormal`). Consequently, the cognitive load to understand the core Monte Carlo logic and overall incidental complexity are equal.
